flashcards.front: What is the primary goal of disk scheduling algorithms?
flashcards.back: To minimize seek time and rotational latency by optimizing the order of I/O requests.

flashcards.front: Which disk scheduling algorithm services requests in their arrival order?
flashcards.back: FCFS (First-Come, First-Served) - simplest but least efficient approach.

flashcards.front: How does SSTF improve upon FCFS?
flashcards.back: Selects request with minimum seek time from current head position, reducing total movement.

flashcards.front: What is the 'elevator algorithm' in disk scheduling?
flashcards.back: SCAN algorithm - moves head in one direction servicing requests, then reverses direction.
C-SCAN returns to start after reaching end (circular motion) instead of reversing direction.

flashcards.front: What RAID level provides striping without redundancy?
flashcards.back: RAID 0 - offers improved performance but no fault tolerance.
RAID 1 - maintains duplicate copies of data on separate disks.

flashcards.front: What is the key feature of RAID 5?
flashcards.back: Block-level striping with distributed parity across all disks.

flashcards.front: How does RAID 10 combine different techniques?
flashcards.back: Combines mirroring (RAID 1) and striping (RAID 0) for performance + redundancy.

flashcards.front: What is rotational latency in HDDs?
flashcards.back: Time for desired sector to rotate under read/write head after seek operation.

flashcards.front: Why is LOOK algorithm more efficient than SCAN?
flashcards.back: Reverses direction immediately when no more requests in current direction.

flashcards.front: What is the main disadvantage of SSTF?
flashcards.back: Starvation risk for requests far from current head position.
